steps.title: Pin down what the conclusion claims
steps.content: The team concluded that anatomical features may affect a dog's ability to distinguish speech from nonspeech. To support this, a finding must link a physical trait (head shape) to the speech-vs-nonspeech distinction — not to language familiarity or language-vs-language discrimination.

steps.title: Find the choice that ties anatomy to speech-vs-nonspeech
steps.content: We need head shape correlated with a larger gap between brain responses to actual language and to the scrambled (nonspeech) recording.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Long-headed dogs accustomed to Spanish... more brain activity than long-headed dogs accustomed to Hungarian': compares languages within one head type; never involves the scrambled nonspeech recording. Misses the speech-vs-nonspeech claim. ✗
  • Choice B — 'longer-headed dogs showed a greater difference in brain activity when hearing either Spanish or Hungarian than when hearing the scrambled recording': directly ties head shape (anatomy) to a larger speech-vs-nonspeech response gap. ✓
  • Choice C — 'pattern... when hearing the scrambled recording was different from the pattern... when hearing the language they were accustomed to': compares mismatched conditions (one group's nonspeech response vs the other group's speech response); doesn't cleanly measure either group's speech-vs-nonspeech distinction. ✗
  • Choice D — 'greater difference... when hearing the language they were accustomed to than when hearing the other language': this is familiar-vs-unfamiliar language, not speech-vs-nonspeech. Wrong distinction. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
